question: Why do you want to study this subject?
lookingFor: Genuine, personal motivation rooted in curiosity — not a generic answer about job prospects.

bullets: Point to a specific moment, experience, or encounter that sparked the interest
Show you've pursued the interest beyond the classroom
Connect the passion to what you want to understand or do
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: It really started in Year 10 when a teacher assigned us a chapter on game theory, and I spent the whole weekend reading about it on my own. I realised I wasn't just interested in the maths — I was fascinated by how formal reasoning could describe the way people actually make decisions. Since then I've read widely around behavioural economics, joined a debating society to stress-test ideas out loud, and that curiosity has only grown. I want to study this because I genuinely cannot stop thinking about the questions it raises.

commonMistakes: Citing career prospects or salary as the main reason
Vague enthusiasm with no concrete origin story
Describing interest that clearly stopped at school and went no further
followUps: What's a question in this field you don't yet know the answer to?

question: What's the topic within your subject that you find most interesting, and why?
lookingFor: Depth of engagement with a specific area — shows the applicant reads beyond the syllabus.

bullets: Name a specific topic, not the whole discipline
Explain what makes it interesting or unresolved
Show you've explored it beyond class — a book, article, experiment, or project
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'm most drawn to epigenetics — the idea that how genes are expressed can be shaped by environment and even passed across generations. What grips me is that it complicates the clean nature-versus-nurture story I was taught, and there are still real scientific debates about how heritable these changes actually are. I read Nessa Carey's book on the topic last summer and came away with more questions than I started with, which is exactly the feeling I want to keep chasing.

commonMistakes: Naming a topic you studied in class but showing no independent engagement with it
Being too broad — 'I find everything interesting' is not an answer
Describing what the topic is without saying why it captivates you
followUps: What's a debate or open question in that area that you'd like to explore?

question: Tell us about a book, article, or podcast you've engaged with recently that relates to your subject.
lookingFor: Evidence of genuine independent inquiry — the applicant reads, listens, or watches beyond what school assigns.

bullets: Name something specific, not just a subject area
Say what it argued or explored — show you actually engaged with it
Reflect on what it changed or confirmed in your thinking
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I recently worked through Richard Feynman's 'The Character of Physical Law' — it's a set of lectures aimed at non-specialists, but he goes into why physical laws have the mathematical form they do, and I found that genuinely surprising. I'd always thought of equations as descriptions, but he argues they reveal something deeper about symmetry and conservation. It made me want to understand Noether's theorem properly, so I've started working through some introductory notes on that. The book changed the question I'm asking when I see a formula.

commonMistakes: Naming a textbook prescribed by school as the thing you chose to read
Summarising the content without any personal reaction or takeaway
Picking something impressive-sounding but being unable to explain it
followUps: Did it challenge anything you already believed?

question: When did you first realise you were seriously interested in this subject — what sparked it?
lookingFor: A specific, authentic origin story that shows the interest is personal and not manufactured for the application.

bullets: Name a moment, person, or experience — be concrete
Show how that spark led to sustained interest, not just a phase
Connect it to where you are now
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: It was a single conversation with my grandfather, who worked as a civil engineer in the 1970s. He described designing a bridge without any computer simulation — just hand calculations and physical intuition. I was fifteen and couldn't believe that was possible. That evening I looked up structural analysis and fell down a rabbit hole I haven't climbed out of since. I started building small load-bearing structures out of balsa wood, and by the time I chose my A-level options I knew there was nowhere else I was going.

commonMistakes: A vague answer like 'I've always loved it' with no specifics
A manufactured story that sounds like every other applicant's answer
Describing the spark but not how it grew into sustained commitment
followUps: How has your understanding of the subject shifted since that first moment?

question: Which part of your studies have you found you're genuinely good at, and how do you know?
lookingFor: Honest self-assessment backed by evidence — not false modesty or empty boasting.

bullets: Name a specific skill or area, not just a subject
Back it with evidence: a result, feedback, or an experience
Show you understand why you're good at it, not just that you are
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think I'm strongest at constructing arguments — taking a messy set of evidence and finding the line that connects them into something coherent. I got consistent feedback from my teachers that my essays have a clearer structure than most, and I noticed the same thing in debating, where I could usually respond quickly to a counter-argument because I'd already mapped the whole space. I think it comes from reading a lot of history, where causation is almost never simple, so I trained myself early to hold multiple explanations at once.

commonMistakes: Claiming broad excellence at everything
A strength with no evidence or example
Confusing 'I got good marks' with actually understanding why you're good at it
followUps: Is that strength useful in this subject, and how?

question: What topic or concept in your subject have you found hardest to grasp, and how did you approach it?
lookingFor: Intellectual honesty about difficulty, paired with a real strategy for working through it.

bullets: Name a specific concept — not just 'maths' or 'the hard parts'
Describe what made it hard
Show the concrete steps you took to understand it, and whether they worked
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Integration by parts tripped me up for weeks — I could follow the worked examples step by step but had no sense of when to use it or how to choose what to call u and dv. Watching my teacher do it wasn't helping, so I went back to first principles: I read the derivation from the product rule until I actually understood where it came from, then tried to solve problems without notes so I had to think rather than copy. It clicked slowly, but what helped most was teaching it to a friend, because explaining it made me find the gaps in my own understanding.

commonMistakes: Naming a difficulty that sounds too easy and isn't credible
Describing the problem with no real attempt at a solution
Implying you simply moved on without resolving it
followUps: What does that tell you about how you learn?

question: Have you noticed any connections between your chosen subject and another area of study? What did you make of them?
lookingFor: Cross-disciplinary thinking — the ability to see structural similarities across fields, which signals deeper intellectual engagement.

bullets: Name a specific connection, not a vague overlap
Explain why the connection is meaningful or surprising
Show what it reveals about one or both subjects
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Studying economics and philosophy together made me notice how much economic modelling relies on assumptions about rationality that philosophers have contested for decades. The expected utility model in economics treats preferences as consistent and stable, but I'd just read about Kahneman's work showing they're neither. What struck me was that two disciplines were studying the same phenomenon — human choice — with almost no shared vocabulary. I found it genuinely exciting that a gap like that still exists and that bridging it is considered live research.

commonMistakes: A surface connection like 'maths is used in science'
Describing the overlap without saying what it reveals or why it matters
Treating interdisciplinary thinking as a box to tick rather than showing genuine curiosity
followUps: Which field do you think has the better framework for understanding that shared problem?

question: Is there a recent development, discovery, or debate in your subject that's caught your attention? Tell us about it.
lookingFor: Active engagement with the field beyond the school curriculum — shows the applicant is already thinking like a practitioner.

bullets: Name something specific and recent — not a decades-old classic
Explain what the development is and why it matters
Give your own take or reaction to it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been following the debate around the replication crisis in psychology — the finding that many landmark studies, including some I was taught in class, can't be reproduced. What I find compelling isn't the scandal of it but what it reveals about how science actually works: publication bias, small sample sizes, p-hacking. It made me think more carefully about how I read any study, and I'm now much more interested in methodology and pre-registration as a result. It's the kind of thing that makes me want to go into research rather than away from it.

commonMistakes: Naming something from the school syllabus rather than a genuine current development
Summarising the development without any personal reaction or analysis
Picking a topic you clearly don't understand well enough to discuss
followUps: What's your own view on it?

question: Tell us about a piece of academic work — a project, essay, or experiment — that you're genuinely proud of.
lookingFor: A specific example that shows intellectual initiative and the ability to do more than answer the question set.

bullets: Describe the work and what it involved — be concrete
Say what made you proud: the thinking, the process, or the result
Show what you learned from doing it
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: For my extended essay I looked at whether there's a statistically significant relationship between a country's income inequality and its life expectancy once you control for GDP. I designed the methodology myself, wrestled with which datasets to trust, and found I had to revise my hypothesis midway through when the data didn't support my original assumption. I'm proud of it not because of the mark but because I felt for the first time like I was asking my own question rather than answering someone else's. The process of changing my mind based on evidence was genuinely exciting.

commonMistakes: Describing a piece of work that was entirely teacher-directed
Focusing only on the grade and not on the thinking
Choosing something low-stakes that doesn't reveal genuine intellectual engagement
followUps: What would you do differently if you could start it again?

subtopic: Applying theory to the real world
question: Can you give an example of a concept from your subject that you've seen play out in the real world?
lookingFor: The ability to move between abstraction and application — a mark of genuine subject understanding rather than rote knowledge.

bullets: Name a specific concept and explain it briefly
Describe a concrete real-world instance you noticed
Say what the connection taught you about either the concept or the situation
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Supply and demand elasticity became real to me during the pandemic petrol shortages. I'd learned the theory in class but watching the price of fuel spike while queues stretched around the block made me think about inelastic demand in a completely different way — people couldn't just stop needing to get to work. What was more interesting was seeing how price caps in some places led to hoarding, exactly what the textbook predicts. It made me realise the theory isn't just a model: it describes pressure that's actually there, whether or not people can name it.

commonMistakes: Describing the concept without connecting it to a specific real example
An example so obvious it shows no independent thinking
Applying the concept incorrectly — showing you don't fully understand it
followUps: Did the real-world case match the theory perfectly, or were there gaps?

question: Has anything you've been taught in school made you think 'I'm not sure that's right'? How did you pursue that?
lookingFor: Intellectual independence and the confidence to question — paired with an honest, evidence-based way of pursuing that doubt.

bullets: Name a specific claim or framing that prompted doubt
Explain why you doubted it and what you did about it
Show what you concluded — even if you ended up agreeing with the original claim
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: We were taught that the First World War was caused by the assassination of Franz Ferdinand, and even then it felt thin to me — one bullet causing four years of war across continents seemed implausible. I read Christopher Clark's 'The Sleepwalkers' alongside a more traditional account, and what I found was that the assignment of blame is genuinely contested among historians and probably shouldn't be settled for a school essay. My conclusion was that the syllabus wasn't wrong exactly, but it was presenting one interpretation as fact. Questioning it made me read more carefully ever since.

commonMistakes: Describing a correction to a factual error rather than genuine intellectual doubt
Criticising without any evidence or follow-up research
Backing down from the doubt to seem agreeable
followUps: What would it take to change your mind on it?

question: Is there a problem or question in your subject you'd love to work on one day?
lookingFor: Intellectual ambition grounded in real knowledge of the field — shows long-term curiosity rather than just a desire for a degree.

bullets: Name a specific problem, not just a vague area
Show you understand why it's unsolved or contested
Connect it authentically to your interests, even if it's very ambitious
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'm fascinated by the problem of antibiotic resistance, specifically whether we can find ways to make bacteria pay a fitness cost for developing resistance so that the mutation doesn't spread as easily. I've read that some researchers are experimenting with phage therapy as a way around it entirely. I'm under no illusion that this is close to a solution, but the fact that it combines evolutionary biology, chemistry, and clinical practice in one problem is exactly the kind of scope I want to work at eventually. Whether I end up at the bench or on the policy side, I want to be near that question.

commonMistakes: Naming a problem you clearly haven't thought about beyond the headlines
Being so vague it could apply to any student in any field
Pretending certainty about a career path that's obviously years away
followUps: What's the first thing you'd need to learn to even begin approaching that problem?

question: Is there a debate or controversy within your subject where you've formed your own view? Walk us through it.
lookingFor: Evidence that the applicant follows live debates in the field and can hold a reasoned position while acknowledging the other side.

bullets: Name the debate specifically — not just 'there are two sides'
Explain both positions fairly before stating your own
Show your view is based on reasoning, not just gut preference
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In economics there's an ongoing argument about whether a universal basic income would increase or decrease work incentives. Opponents say it removes the pressure to work; proponents point to the pilot studies showing people mostly continue working and use the security to take better jobs. My reading of the Finnish and Kenyan pilots is that the effect on employment is modest but the effect on mental health and risk-taking is significant, and I think we're measuring the wrong outcome. My view is that UBI is more interesting as a safety-net redesign than as a labour-market intervention, but I hold that view lightly because the evidence base is still thin.

commonMistakes: Presenting only one side rather than genuinely engaging with the other
A view that's just the consensus — where's the thinking?
Picking a controversy that's actually settled and presenting it as live
followUps: What evidence would change your mind?

question: Have you pursued your interest in this subject in any way outside of school? What did you do?
lookingFor: Self-directed engagement that shows the interest is intrinsic, not just about passing exams.

bullets: Name at least one concrete activity — online course, club, project, work experience
Describe what you actually learned or did, not just that you attended
Show how it deepened or changed your understanding
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I completed a free online course on linear algebra from MIT OpenCourseWare during the summer — not because I had to, but because I kept hitting the edges of what I understood when reading about machine learning, and I wanted to fill the gap properly. I also run a small maths blog where I try to explain one topic a week as clearly as I can, because writing forces me to find where my understanding is actually shallow. The blog started as a personal exercise but a few people read it now, which has made me more careful about accuracy.

commonMistakes: Counting homework or exam revision as pursuing the subject outside school
Listing activities without describing any real engagement or learning
An activity clearly done just to put on the application
followUps: What's the most surprising thing you discovered through that outside work?

question: Tell us about a time an academic task didn't go the way you hoped. What did you learn from it?
lookingFor: Honest reflection on an academic setback with a constructive response — shows maturity and a growth orientation.

bullets: Describe a real setback, not a disguised success
Take ownership without blaming external factors
Say concretely what changed in how you work or think
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I submitted a history essay I thought was strong and got back a mark that genuinely shocked me — my argument was there, but I'd ignored two counterarguments the marking criteria required me to address. I'd mistaken confidence for thoroughness. I went back and read the mark scheme properly for the first time, then redid the essay as an exercise rather than for credit. The rewrite was substantially better, and ever since I plan every essay by listing the objections to my argument before I start writing. It's the best habit I've built as a student.

commonMistakes: A setback that isn't actually a failure — a humble-brag in disguise
Attributing the failure mainly to the teacher or the marking
No concrete change in behaviour as a result
followUps: How do you spot now when you're repeating that pattern?

question: What do you think will be the biggest difference between studying this subject at school and studying it at university?
lookingFor: A realistic understanding of what undergraduate study demands — shows the applicant is genuinely prepared for the transition.

bullets: Name a specific difference, not just 'more work'
Show you understand the shift in how knowledge is generated and questioned at university level
Reflect on how you're preparing or what excites you about that shift
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The biggest change, I think, is that school asks me to understand what's been established, while university will ask me to engage with what's still contested. In chemistry, for example, I've learned reliable methods and clean outcomes. I've been told university experiments often don't work first time, that you have to design your own controls, and that real results are ambiguous. I find that genuinely exciting rather than daunting — I've been slightly bored by the certainty of school science for a while now and I want to be in the space where the answers aren't written in the back of a textbook.

commonMistakes: Saying only 'more freedom' or 'more work' without specifics
Showing no understanding of how knowledge actually works at research level
Sounding anxious about the transition in a way that signals unreadiness
followUps: Is there anything about that transition you're actively preparing for?

question: Has any topic or subject surprised you — something you expected to dislike but ended up finding interesting?
lookingFor: Intellectual openness and the ability to update initial assumptions — a quality universities value in learners.

bullets: Be honest about the initial resistance or low expectations
Describe the moment or reason the attitude shifted
Show what you got out of it and whether it changed how you approach new subjects
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I signed up for philosophy as a soft option and expected it to feel vague compared to my sciences. The first session on the problem of induction — whether we can ever logically justify saying the sun will rise tomorrow based on past experience — genuinely unsettled me. I hadn't expected a question with no clean answer to feel urgent. I've since applied that scepticism to how I read scientific papers, and it's made me a more careful thinker across everything. I'm grateful I didn't get to choose my timetable that year.

commonMistakes: A surprise that's too minor to be interesting — 'I liked geography more than expected'
No real explanation of what changed or why
Using the answer to sneak in a subject you already loved
followUps: Did it change how you approach subjects you think you'll dislike?

question: How do you tend to approach learning something new and difficult in your subject?
lookingFor: Self-aware, effective learning strategies — shows the applicant can manage their own education at university level.

bullets: Describe a real process, not a generic 'I work hard'
Give an example of this strategy in action
Show you reflect on what works and adjust when it doesn't
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: When something isn't clicking I first try to find the simplest version of the idea — strip away the context and ask what the core claim actually is. If that doesn't help, I look for a different explanation rather than re-reading the same source, because I've learned that a different author often frames things in a way that connects to something I already understand. Then I try to generate my own examples before I look at worked ones. It's slower than just copying methods, but when it works I actually own the concept. I've refined this over years of noticing what left me stuck on exams versus what didn't.

commonMistakes: Describing passive strategies like re-reading notes
A generic answer with no personal reflection or example
Claiming a process you clearly can't demonstrate in the interview
followUps: Has that approach ever failed you, and what did you do?

question: Is there a thinker, scientist, or practitioner in your field whose work you've found particularly compelling?
lookingFor: Evidence that the applicant has engaged with the intellectual tradition of their subject — they know not just facts but the people who shaped the field.

bullets: Name someone specific and explain what they contributed
Say what drew you to their work in particular
Show some critical engagement — what you find compelling and perhaps where you have questions
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been reading a lot of Hannah Arendt, particularly 'The Origins of Totalitarianism'. What I find compelling is the way she traces how totalitarian movements emerged from real social and political conditions rather than treating them as pure evil that appeared from nowhere. It's a framework I find intellectually useful but also uncomfortable, because it implies these conditions could recur. I don't agree with every argument — some of her categories feel too rigid — but the seriousness with which she treats political catastrophe as something that needs explaining rather than just condemning has changed how I think about political history entirely.

commonMistakes: Naming someone famous but having nothing specific to say about their work
Pure admiration with no critical engagement
Choosing someone you've only read about, not actually read
followUps: Where do you disagree with them, if anywhere?

question: Can you explain a concept from your subject to someone who knows nothing about it?
lookingFor: Depth of understanding — you only truly understand something when you can explain it without jargon.

bullets: Choose a concept you genuinely understand well
Strip out the jargon and use analogy or a concrete image
Check your own explanation — if it only works for experts, you haven't got there yet
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'll try natural selection. Imagine a population of mice living in a field that's become snowier over time. The mice vary in colour — some are darker, some are lighter. Owls pick off the darker ones more easily because they stand out against the snow. The lighter mice survive longer, reproduce more, and their offspring tend to be lighter too. Over many generations the whole population shifts towards being lighter — not because any mouse decided to change, but because survival was unequal. That's natural selection: inherited variation plus unequal survival plus time. No designer required.

commonMistakes: Choosing a concept you half-understand and getting lost partway through
Slipping back into technical language that would lose a non-specialist
An explanation so simplified it's actually wrong
followUps: What's the hardest part of that concept to get across simply?

question: Are there any ethical questions raised by your subject that you find yourself thinking about?
lookingFor: Awareness that academic disciplines have real-world moral implications — shows a rounded, socially conscious intellect.

bullets: Name a specific ethical question, not just 'there are ethical issues'
Show you've thought through why it's genuinely difficult
Give your tentative view while acknowledging the complexity
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: In computer science, the question I keep returning to is who bears responsibility when an algorithm causes harm. If a hiring model trained on biased historical data systematically disadvantages certain applicants, the company might argue the algorithm is just reflecting past data. But that feels like a moral dodge — the designers chose what data to use, what proxy variables to optimise for. I don't think the answer is to abandon algorithmic decision-making, but I do think it requires engineers to be trained in the ethics of their choices, not just the mechanics. I want to be in a position one day to design systems that someone has actually thought that through.

commonMistakes: A generic answer about 'technology can be used for good or bad'
Refusing to take any position because the issue is complex
Describing an ethical concern in another field, not your own subject
followUps: What would you want to do about it if you were in a position to act?

question: What other subject or field do you think most enriches your understanding of what you want to study?
lookingFor: Intellectual breadth and the ability to see how disciplines inform each other — a quality that predicts strong independent thinking at university.

bullets: Name the other subject and the specific way it enriches your main interest
Give a concrete example of where they have intersected for you
Show genuine engagement with both, not just a passing mention
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: History of science has probably done more for my understanding of biology than any second biology course could. Seeing how the theory of evolution was resisted, revised, and eventually accepted — and understanding the social and institutional pressures involved — gave me a much more realistic picture of how scientific knowledge actually progresses. It's not a steady accumulation of facts; it's a process full of resistance, priority disputes, and paradigm shifts. I find I read current papers more critically now because I understand that consensus is hard won and sometimes provisional.

commonMistakes: Naming a subject that's essentially the same as your main subject
A connection that's superficial — 'maths is useful in physics'
No concrete example of where the enrichment actually happened
followUps: Has studying both ever created tension for you, or do they always reinforce each other?

question: Do you find you learn better working on your own or with others? Why?
lookingFor: Genuine self-awareness about learning style, with the maturity to recognise value in both modes.

bullets: Give an honest answer rather than the perceived 'right' answer
Back it with an example from each mode
Show you can adapt rather than being rigid
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My honest answer is that I need both, but at different stages. I think better alone when I'm first working through something — conversations interrupt a process that I haven't completed yet. But once I have a rough understanding, talking it through with someone is invaluable, because I always find holes in my reasoning that I couldn't see while I was inside it. A study group at school showed me this clearly: I dreaded them until we agreed to arrive having worked through the material individually first. After that they became the most useful part of my revision.

commonMistakes: Claiming to love collaborative work in every situation — interviewers can probe this
Giving a rigid answer that implies you can only work one way
No example to ground the claim
followUps: Is there a type of academic task where you'd always choose one over the other?

question: What question about your subject are you most curious about right now — something you don't yet know the answer to?
lookingFor: Genuine intellectual appetite — the applicant has open questions they are actively living with, not just completed topics.

bullets: Name a specific open question, not a topic
Show you understand why it's genuinely open or contested
Convey real curiosity rather than a rehearsed answer
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I keep coming back to the question of whether mathematical structures are discovered or invented. When Riemannian geometry was developed in the nineteenth century as a purely abstract system, it seemed like a mathematical curiosity — and then it turned out to be exactly the framework Einstein needed for general relativity. That correspondence feels too close to be a coincidence, but I can't fully articulate why. It sits at the edge of mathematics and philosophy of mind, and I find I can't put it down even though I have no answer. I'd like to do enough mathematics and philosophy to at least understand what the real arguments look like.

commonMistakes: An open 'question' that's actually just a topic or a broad theme
A question you clearly could answer with a ten-minute search
No sense of genuine personal investment — it sounds like a question you read somewhere
followUps: What would it mean to you to find an answer?

subtopic: What you hope to gain from the degree
question: What do you hope to get out of studying this subject at university — beyond the qualification?
lookingFor: A thoughtful picture of intellectual and personal growth the applicant is seeking — shows the degree is a means to something real, not just a credential.

bullets: Go beyond 'a good job' or 'career prospects'
Name something about how you want to think, what questions you want to live with, or who you want to become
Make it feel personal and specific to your relationship with the subject
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I want to become someone who can hold a complex problem long enough to actually understand it. At school, almost every task has a fixed endpoint — a deadline, a mark scheme, a right answer. What I'm hoping for at university is the experience of sitting with a question that doesn't have a clean answer and developing the patience and method to make progress on it anyway. I also want to be around people who find the same things interesting and will push back on my thinking, because I've learned I need that friction to sharpen my ideas. The qualification matters, but what I'd consider failure is leaving without having become a genuinely more rigorous thinker.

commonMistakes: An answer that's entirely about career outcomes
Vague language about 'growth' with no substance
An answer that could apply to any degree at any university
followUps: How will you know at the end whether you got what you came for?
